Document [16] (Title: Lymphocytosis -- Treatment / Management) CLL: The management of CLL has moved towards the use of targeted therapies such as BTK inhibitors (Ibrutinib), BCL-2 inhibitor (Venetoclax), and PI3K inhibitors (Idelalisib). In general, the first step in CLL management is by appropriate selection of patients needing therapy. These are patients with progressive cytopenias, constitutional symptoms, progressive or symptomatic lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly. The second important step in CLL management relies on risk stratification and predictive factors. Particularly, the presence of 17p del or TP53 mutation predicts for lack of response from standard chemoimmunotherapy with fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab (FCR) or bendamustine plus rituximab (BR). These patients are better suited for the use of BTK inhibitors (Ibrutinib) or Venetoclax in combination with the humanized anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody obinutuzumab. The phase III ECOG-E1912 has proved the superiority of the combination of Ibrutinib and rituximab (IbRx) over the FCR regimen in younger patients (age less than 70 years) diagnosed with CLL without del17 mutation. The progression-free survival (89.4% in the IbRx arm vs. 72.9% in the FCR arm at 3 years; hazard ratio for progression or death, 0.35; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.22 to 0.56; P<0.001)and overall survival (98.8% in the IbRx arm vs. 91.5% in FCR arm at 3 years; hazard ratio for death, 0.17; 95% CI, 0.05 to 0.54; P<0.001) were significantly prolonged in patients diagnosed with CLL, even in those with IGHV mutation. [18]
Document [17] (Title: Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management -- Mantle Cell Lymphoma) Treatment of the less aggressive disease is with either R- CHOP or R- Benda, followed by maintenance rituximab. Most patients also need more intensive treatment regimens. One agent cytarabine is included in most regimens due to its activity in mantle cell lymphoma. The front line chemotherapy is followed in the first remission by consolidation with high dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplantation. One such approach is the Nordic protocol (a maxi R–CHOP regimen alternating with rituximab and high-dose cytarabine). Another regimen is alternating hyper CVAD (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin, dexamethasone) with methotrexate/cytarabine. Lenalidomide and bortezomib have single-agent activity in relapsed MCL. The Bruton’s tyrosine kinase inhibitor, Ibrutinib, is approved by the FDA for use in relapsed MCL as it has good activity in MCL. [14]

Document [22] (Title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- D. Relapsed/Refractory CLL) Ibrutinib is the first-line therapy to improve progression-free survival and response to treatment in relapsed/refractory CLL.  Other drugs of choice for relapsed CLL include idelalisib (a phosphoinositide 3'-kinase [PI3K] delta inhibitor), Alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody directed against CD52, a cellular marker found in CLL) and venetoclax (Bcl-2 inhibitor). Venetoclax is actively used in refractory CLL patients with 17p deletion. Venetoclax induces brisk apoptosis and occasionally induces tumor lysis syndrome. Anti-CD19 chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has also been active in refractory cases. Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (rituximab, ofatumumab, obinutuzumab) may briefly alleviate symptoms in relapsed/refractory CLL patients.

Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.
Document [2] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81) Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.
Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 98) The community’s knowledge base defines leukemia (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a hematologic malignancy comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—share the mechanism of inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase fusion protein and are assigned to specific subtypes: dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Additional anti‑leukemic agents include the corticosteroid derivative kortizon acetát, dexamethasone phosphate, the topoisomerase‑I inhibitor irinotecan hydrochloride, the antimetabolite methotrexate sodium, the alkylating agent pipobroman, and several prednisolone salts (prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate). Conversely, the oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec is contraindicated in patients with any form of leukemia. These relationships support precise drug selection and comprehensive management of hematologic oncology patients.

Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 240) BCR‑ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Dasatinib, its anhydrous form, imatinib, and imatinib mesylát are recognized as instances of the BCR‑ABL Inhibitor class and have demonstrated efficacy in treating أبيضاض الدم اللمفاوي (lymphoid leukemia). Understanding these specific drug‑disease relationships helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapy based on the leukemia subtype.
